epigraph.quote: The script is not a blueprint. It is a score. The character is not written — the character is implied by the silences between the lines. Your job is to hear the music before you play a note.

blocks.text: INT. LIBRARY — NIGHT
A script on the table. Face down. Brother Jack sits across from THE APPRENTICE.
BROTHER JACK
Turn it over. Read it. All of it. Without stopping. Without making notes. Without deciding anything.
a beat
Let it arrive the way the audience will receive it. In order. With surprise.
Where does your breath change? Mark those places. Not the dramatic moments. The places your breath actually changes. Those are where the scene lives.
She reads. He watches her read.
BROTHER JACK
after she finishes
Where did your breath change?
She tells him. The places are not where she expected.
INT. LIBRARY — CONTINUOUS
BROTHER JACK
Every script you ever read — five questions. In this order.
a beat
What does my character want in this scene? Not in the play. In this scene. Specifically.
a beat
What is stopping them from getting it?
a beat
What do they do about it?
a beat
What changes by the end of the scene?
a beat
What do they never say — but the audience must hear?
a long beat
The fifth question is the hardest. And the most important.
The thing never said is the scene's actual content.
Everything else is the surface.
INT. LIBRARY — LATER
BROTHER JACK
Silence in a script is not empty.
a beat
It is the most loaded moment the writer can give you.
Because the audience fills silence with everything they know about your character.
Which means the silence can only be as full as your preparation.
a beat
An actor with a shallow character produces an empty silence.
An actor with a fully excavated character produces a silence the audience cannot stop feeling.
the close
The next lesson will build that character.
From the wound up.
Keep the Five Questions. Not as a formula. As a lens. Apply them to every script you read for the rest of your life. They will never stop revealing something you missed.
The fifth question — what does the character never say — is the entire work of Phase Three Lessons Four and Five. Begin listening for it now.

requirement.prompt: Post the Five Questions applied to a scene you are currently working on or one you choose. Answer all five publicly. The fifth especially. Include #JoelhoodP3L1.
